figures.caption: Fig. 1: Approach overview. We present AnchorD as a training-free method for grounding monocular depth predictions (MDE) using real-world sensor depth using a factor graph approach, which yields grounded, highly-accurate depth predictions on typical non-Lambertian objects.

Fig. 7: Qualitative results on SprayD. We compare AnchorD (ours) with metric Depth Anything3 [27] as well as affine scaling in the three rightmost columns. For reference, we display the raw sensor depth and ground truth depth in the two leftmost columns. All depth images along each row feature a consistent color scaling.

TABLE II: Evaluation on SprayD. We report the Mean Absolute Error (MAE) (↓) in meters, the Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) (↓) and the relative error (REL) (↓). We distinguish between the depth values of objects and the background and report the full errors over all pixels. Best results are in bold, second best are underlined. We observe that DAV3-metric [27] is already outperformed by affine alignment and even further by AnchorD.

Fig. 5: Factor graph formulation. Dense per-pixel depth variables Dij and patch-wise affine parameters (si, bi) are jointly optimized. Ternary MDE factors ϕmde align depth to the monocular prediction within each patch, unary sensor factors ϕsen enforce metric consistency, and binary logarithmic slope factors ϕslp preserve relative depth structure across pixels and patch boundaries. For simplicity, only four pixels per patch are shown.
